'''Kunitsukami''' were the kami of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref>
== References ==

'''Kunitsukami''' were the kami of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often represented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>
== References ==

'''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often represented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>
== References ==

'''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often represented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>
== References ==

'''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often represented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>
== List of kunitsukami ==
* Ok
== References ==

'''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often represented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>

'''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>

{{AfC submission|||ts=20210926190106|u=CycoMa|ns=118}}

{{AfC submission|T}} '''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>
Many myths in the Nixon Shoki and the Kojiki are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&q=Earth+kami&hl=en|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT75&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
== List of kunitsukami ==
* [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&pg=PA440&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiaqNahjZvzAhWBX80KHf1PC3M4KBDoAXoECAQQAw|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440-441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi]] *[[Tenazuchi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA7&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=8|language=en}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
{{AfC submission|||ts=20210926190106|u=CycoMa|ns=118}}

{{AfC submission|T}} '''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref>
Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&q=Earth+kami&hl=en|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT75&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
== List of kunitsukami ==
* [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&pg=PA440&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiaqNahjZvzAhWBX80KHf1PC3M4KBDoAXoECAQQAw|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440-441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA7&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=8|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{reflist}}
{{AfC submission|||ts=20210926190106|u=CycoMa|ns=118}}

{{AfC submission|T}} '''Kunitsukami''' were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.
Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&q=Earth+kami&hl=en|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT75&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>

{{AfC submission|T}} '''Kunitsukami''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last=Klautau|first=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA130&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA28&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) were the [[kami]] of the land.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT18&dq=kunitsukami+dictionary&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwinnJynv5bzAhVRKFkFHZPlBvkQ6AF6BAgKEAM|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> They were contrasted with the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":2" /> Although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land. Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&q=Earth+kami&hl=en|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT75&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
== List of kunitsukami ==
* [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&pg=PA440&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiaqNahjZvzAhWBX80KHf1PC3M4KBDoAXoECAQQAw|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440-441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA7&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=8|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA76&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&hl=en|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&q=Earth+kami&hl=en|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT75&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
== Mythology == Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />

B. Picken]] said that distinction isn’t clear.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA76&dq=earth+kami&hl=en|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=147|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA7&dq=kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiN6KucyZbzAhWNKFkFHbg4Bs8Q6AF6BAgDEAM|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&q=Earth+kami&hl=en|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT75&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
== Mythology == Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami ==
* [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&pg=PA440&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiaqNahjZvzAhWBX80KHf1PC3M4KBDoAXoECAQQAw|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440-441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA7&dq=Kunitsukami&hl=en|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=8|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA76&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&hl=en|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
== Mythology == Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />

They were contrasted from the [[Amatsukami]],<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and [[Karl Florenz]], kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami ==
* [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=8|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}} [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Shinto}} '''Kunitsukami''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in tsuchi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the [[Amatsukami]],<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>

{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as [[Kunitsukami]],<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />

== References == {{reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Types of deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]]

{{Shinto}} '''Kunitsukami''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in tsuchi.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the [[Amatsukami]],<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as [[Kunitsukami]],<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *{{Interlanguage link|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|lt=Ashinazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|simple}}<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *{{Interlanguage link|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|lt=Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|simple}}<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], {{ill|Omizunu|ja|淤美豆奴神}}, {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Takiribime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[Aesir]] and [[Vanir]] * [[Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[Tudigong]] ** [[City God (China)|City God]] *{{ill|Kunitama Okami|lt=Okunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|ja|国魂}}

The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. <ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) revision.text:
{{Shinto}} '''Kunitsukami''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the [[Amatsukami]],<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. <ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}.<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all [[Kunitsukami]] or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as [[Kunitsukami]],<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Takiribime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[Aesir]] and [[Vanir]] * [[Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}}
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Kunitsukami}}
[[Category:Kunitsukami| ]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Types of deities]] [[Category:History of Shinto]]

revision.comment: If [[Amatsukami]] has an Italic title, so should this article.

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Takiribime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}

{{Shinto}} {{italic title}} '''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Takiribime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[Aesir]] and [[Vanir]] * [[Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}}

revision.text:
{{Shinto}} {{italic title}} '''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[Aesir]] and [[Vanir]] * [[Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}}

They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[Aesir]] and [[Vanir]] * [[Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}}

{{Shinto}} {{italic title}} '''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三}} proposed a system justifying [[Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[Aesir]] and [[Vanir]] * [[Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}}

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[ Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[Toyotama-hime]], [[Omizunu]], {{Ill|Chikato no Kami|ja|千鹿頭神}}, [[Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|イチキシマヒメ}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[Aesir]] and [[Vanir]] * [[Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
