{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|qq=|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|qq=|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and [[:en:Vanir|Vanir]] * [[:en:Houtu|Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[:en:City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and [[:en:Vanir|Vanir]] * [[:en:Houtu|Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[:en:City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>

The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />

== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Kunitsukami]] [[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]] [[zh:國神]]
{{wikidata link|Q60995523}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and [[:en:Vanir|Vanir]] * [[:en:Houtu|Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[:en:City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />

== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and [[:en:Vanir|Vanir]] * [[:en:Houtu|Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[:en:City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and [[:en:Vanir|Vanir]] * [[:en:Houtu|Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[:en:City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

{{wikidata link|Q60995523}} [[Category:History of Shinto]] [[Category:Kunitsukami| ]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Types of deities]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as [[:en:Tutelary deity|tutelary deities]]. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to [[:en:Ernest Mason Satow|Ernest Mason Satow]] and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />

* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], [[:en:Kuebiko|Kuebiko]], Taka Kagyu, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]], [[:en:Omizunu|Omizunu]], {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and [[:en:Vanir|Vanir]] * [[:en:Houtu|Houtu]] ** [[Landlord deity]] ** [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] / [[Ujigami]] ** [[:en:City God (China)|City God]] *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
