revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deities}}. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and {{ill|Vanir|en|Vanir|WD=Q184826|lt=Vanir}} * {{ill|Houtu|en|Houtu|WD=Q3235126|lt=Houtu}} ** [[Landlord deity]] ** {{ill|Tudigong|en|Tudigong|WD=Q2670522|lt=Tudigong}} / [[Ujigami]] ** {{ill|City God (China)|en|City God (China)|WD=Q702182|lt=City God}} *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}}
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Kunitsukami}}

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deities}}. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and {{ill|Vanir|en|Vanir|WD=Q184826|lt=Vanir}} * {{ill|Houtu|en|Houtu|WD=Q3235126|lt=Houtu}} ** [[Landlord deity]] ** {{ill|Tudigong|en|Tudigong|WD=Q2670522|lt=Tudigong}} / [[Ujigami]] ** {{ill|City God (China)|en|City God (China)|WD=Q702182|lt=City God}} *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}{{More categories|date=November 2021}}
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Kunitsukami}}

According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deities}}. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and {{ill|Vanir|en|Vanir|WD=Q184826|lt=Vanir}} * {{ill|Houtu|en|Houtu|WD=Q3235126|lt=Houtu}} ** [[Landlord deity]] ** {{ill|Tudigong|en|Tudigong|WD=Q2670522|lt=Tudigong}} / [[Ujigami]] ** {{ill|City God (China)|en|City God (China)|WD=Q702182|lt=City God}} *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deities}}. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and {{ill|Vanir|en|Vanir|WD=Q184826|lt=Vanir}} * {{ill|Houtu|en|Houtu|WD=Q3235126|lt=Houtu}} ** [[Landlord deity]] ** {{ill|Tudigong|en|Tudigong|WD=Q2670522|lt=Tudigong}} / [[Ujigami]] ** {{ill|City God (China)|en|City God (China)|WD=Q702182|lt=City God}} *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deities|1=Tutelary deity}}. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow|1=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu|1=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and {{ill|Vanir|en|Vanir|WD=Q184826|lt=Vanir|1=Vanir}} * {{ill|Houtu|en|Houtu|WD=Q3235126|lt=Houtu|1=Houtu}} ** [[Landlord deity]] ** {{ill|Tudigong|en|Tudigong|WD=Q2670522|lt=Tudigong|1=Tudigong}} / [[Ujigami]] ** {{ill|City God (China)|en|City God (China)|WD=Q702182|lt=City God|1=City God (China)}} *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu|1=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and {{ill|Vanir|en|Vanir|WD=Q184826|lt=Vanir|1=Vanir}} * {{ill|Houtu|en|Houtu|WD=Q3235126|lt=Houtu|1=Houtu}} ** [[Landlord deity]] ** {{ill|Tudigong|en|Tudigong|WD=Q2670522|lt=Tudigong|1=Tudigong}} / [[Ujigami]] ** {{ill|City God (China)|en|City God (China)|WD=Q702182|lt=City God|1=City God (China)}} *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kunitsukami]][[fr:Kunitsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[zh:國神]] {{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|WD=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deities|1=Tutelary deity}}. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|WD=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow|1=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|WD=Q2329261|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|WD=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu|1=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[Tsumi|Heavenly and Earthly crimes]] * [[:en:Aesir|Aesir]] and {{ill|Vanir|en|Vanir|WD=Q184826|lt=Vanir|1=Vanir}} * {{ill|Houtu|en|Houtu|WD=Q3235126|lt=Houtu|1=Houtu}} ** [[Landlord deity]] ** {{ill|Tudigong|en|Tudigong|WD=Q2670522|lt=Tudigong|1=Tudigong}} / [[Ujigami]] ** {{ill|City God (China)|en|City God (China)|WD=Q702182|lt=City God|1=City God (China)}} *[[Kunitama|Okunitama]]

== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|qid=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|qid=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|qid=Q2329261|qid=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|qid=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu|1=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{Shinto}} {{italic title}} ''''Kunitsukami''''' (国つ神''','''<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last1=Klautau|first1=Orion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OjcWEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA130|title=Buddhism and Modernity: Sources from Nineteenth-Century Japan|last2=Krämer|first2=Hans Martin|date=2021-03-31|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-8458-1|pages=130|language=en}}</ref> 国津神<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA28|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|pages=28|language=en}}</ref>) are the [[kami]] of the land<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MWGQAgAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami+dictionary&pg=PT18|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005-09-30|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-79738-6|language=en}}</ref> and live in ''tsuchi'' (earth).<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=38|language=en}}</ref>
They were contrasted from the ''[[Amatsukami]]'',<ref name=":2" /> although modern [[Shinto]] no longer makes the distinction between Amatsukami and Kunitsukami.<ref>{{Cite web|title=kami {{!}} Definition, Translation, & Facts|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/kami|access-date=2021-09-28|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref> According to [[Yijiang Zhong]] the distinction was made by the writers of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki to formulate a political discourse.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LrrMDAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami|title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo|date=2016-10-06|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7109-7|language=en}}</ref>
Kunitsukami were often presented as {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|qid=Q3032312|lt=tutelary deities|1=Tutelary deity}}. They were also associated with geographical areas along with their inhabitants. Non-royal families also viewed them as their ancestors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Drott|first=Edward R.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=kunitsukami&pg=PA7|title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan|date=2016-04-30|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7|pages=7|language=en}}</ref> They also were considered personifications of the land.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&q=Earth+kami|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|pages=141|language=en}}</ref>
According to {{ill|Ernest Mason Satow|en|Ernest Mason Satow|qid=Q166598|lt=Ernest Mason Satow|1=Ernest Mason Satow}} and {{ill|Karl Florenz|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karl Florenz|qq=}}, kunitsukami might have been deified chiefs who migrated to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Satow|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XtsJBAAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PT75|title=Ancient Japanese Rituals|date=2014-07-16|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-79291-8|language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>
The {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} [[Kunitama|Ōkunitama]], [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|61}}<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal |last1=Kōji |first1=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042 |jstor=27822899}}</ref>{{rp|53-54}} They are all Kunitsukami or earthly kami representing the land.<ref name=":03" />{{rp|53-54}}
{{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|lt=Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q29410596}} proposed a system justifying [[:en:Japanese Imperialism|Japanese Imperialism]] where Japanese people in the colonies were seen as [[Amatsukami]] and natives were seen as Kunitsukami,<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Kōji |first=Suga |last2=𳜳𨀉𠄈 |date=2010 |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822899 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=47–74 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> however he was later censored as his position was considered too supportive of the rights of colonized peoples.<ref name=":02" />
== Mythology == {{Expand section|date=October 2021}} Many myths in the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]] are about the conflict between the Kunitsukami and the [[Amatsukami]].<ref name=":0" />
== List of kunitsukami == {{Incomplete list|date=November 2021}} * [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Narayanan|first=Vasudha|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA440|title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places|date=2005|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-522191-6|pages=440–441|language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&q=Kunitsukami&pg=PA7 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en}}</ref> *[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Tenazuchi]]<ref name=":1" /> *[[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name=":0" /> *[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&q=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
* Sovereign God ** [[Ōkuninushi]] * Ōkuninushi no Gokojin ** [[Ajisukitakahikone]], Shimo-shitsu-biki, [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], [[Takeminakata]], Kizumata god, and Tora-kami god * The gods who are the spouses of the lord of the great nation ** Suseri Vipassana, Yagami Vipassana, Numagawa Vipassana, Dokiri Vipassana, Kamiya Taten Vipassana, Tottorijin * Others ** [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], [[Kushinadahime]], [[Sukunabikona]], [[Ōmononushi]], {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|qid=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}}, Taka Kagyu, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|qid=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}, [[Ōyamatsumi]], [[Ukanomitama]], [[Toshigami]], [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|qid=Q2329261|qid=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}}, {{ill|Omizunu|en|Omizunu|qid=Q48745416|lt=Omizunu|1=Omizunu}}, {{ill|Chikatō-no-Kami|ja|千鹿頭神|1=Chikatō-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatō-no-Kami|qq=}}, [[Moriya|Moreya]], [[Isetsuhiko]], {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}
